topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyers for the new city plastered the bulletin board, mocking me with their bright, optimistic colors. I traced the outline of my thumb over the address, the ink blurring slightly under the pressure. Liam. Moving. Across the continent. A cold knot tightened in my chest, a physical pressure making it hard to draw a full breath. I watched him across the crowded coffee shop, his laughter bright and easy, completely oblivious to the seismic shift happening within me. When he finally looked up, his smile faltered for a fraction of a second, a flicker of something I couldn't quite decipher, before he smoothly redirected the conversation. I just nodded, the movement stiff, and ordered my lukewarm tea, the bitterness mirroring the taste of the sudden emptiness in my mouth.

[story 2]

I sat on the edge of my bed, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. The packing tape lay on the floor like a defeated snake. Every box represented a memory I was actively trying to shove into a corner, a place where I wouldn't have to face the reality of his absence. I picked up an old photograph—us, grinning foolishly on a beach trip—and the edges felt rough beneath my fingertips. It wasn't sadness, exactly; it was a heavy, leaden feeling settling deep in my bones, making my limbs feel weighted down. When my phone buzzed with an email confirming his departure date, I didn't move to open it. I just stared at the ceiling, focusing intently on a crack in the plaster, willing myself to become invisible.

[story 3]

"So, you're really doing it?" The words sounded thin and brittle in my own ears. I kept my hands clasped so tightly around my mug that my knuckles were white, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to the present moment. He spoke about new opportunities, about adventure, and I managed a weak, practiced chuckle that felt entirely foreign. Inside, a hollow space opened up where anticipation used to reside. It felt like watching a tide retreat from the shore, leaving behind only damp sand and the unsettling awareness of exposure. I found myself staring out the window at the familiar street, cataloging every detail, trying to imprint the sight onto my retina before it vanished, just as I feared everything familiar was about to.
